commands.author: clawsweeper[bot]
commands.repair_reason: - No automated repair should run because the remaining blocker is maintainer selection of the canonical FTS design and acceptance of the upgrade shape, not a narrow code defect.; Cleared: No concrete security or supply-chain concern was found; the diff changes local memory indexing/schema/tests and adds no dependencies, workflows, downloads, permissions, or secret handling. (sha=2653f72688fe5058f4ae9e3a5bd000189745810f)
commands.review_summary:
This PR adds a separate derived path FTS table for memory indexes, writes/searches/repairs/publishes it from canonical memory chunks, and expands memory schema/search regression coverage.

PR surface: Source +289, Tests +721. Total +1010 across 10 files.

Reproducibility: yes. The linked issue includes exact `openclaw memory search` output, and current main still stores paths as unindexed FTS metadata while inserting only chunk body text into the indexed FTS text column.
commands.review_followup: - No automated repair should run because the remaining blocker is maintainer selection of the canonical FTS design and acceptance of the upgrade shape, not a narrow code defect.

target.title: fix(memory-core): include path in FTS indexed text so filename tokens are searchable
target.body:
## What problem does this PR solve?

`openclaw memory search` can rank filename/date-token queries poorly or miss them when the filename token only lives in the derived path FTS index. The current PR was also stale against the canonical `memory_index_*` table migration, so the original fix needed to be rebased onto the current schema owner instead of keeping the old `chunks_*` wiring.

## Why does this matter now?

The linked issue is a user-visible memory lookup failure: a clean memory index and working content search still did not reliably return the expected memory file for a filename/date-token query such as `2026-06-17-1649`. Without this repair, users can have the right memory file indexed but still fail to retrieve it by the filename/date token they know.

## What is the intended outcome?

Filename/date-token keyword searches should find the matching memory file while body-text BM25 ranking remains based on body text only. `memory_index_chunks` remains canonical, and both derived FTS tables are repaired or published from that canonical table.

## What is intentionally out of scope?

This does not change memory file format, vector search scoring, providers, auth, network behavior, gateway startup behavior, or the public memory API contract.

## Summary

- Fix classification: root-cause compatibility and reliability repair for memory FTS path search on the current canonical memory index schema.
- Maintainer-ready confidence: high for the local code path because the current head has real CLI proof for the filename/date-token lookup, keeps regressions for search merging/schema repair/shadow publish behavior, and re-runs ClawSweeper clean after the FTS repair-marker update.
- Root cause: filename/date-token matches live in a derived path FTS index, but search/schema/publish paths were not consistently treating `memory_index_chunks` as the source of truth for both `memory_index_chunks_fts` and `memory_index_chunks_fts_path`. The first repair also checked same-count drift by scanning FTS virtual tables on unindexed metadata columns during every schema initialization.
- What changed: memory indexing now writes both the body FTS table and the separate path FTS table; stale cleanup removes both derived FTS rows; schema repair rebuilds missing or unmarked derived FTS rows from `memory_index_chunks`; full shadow reindex publishing copies `memory_index_chunks_fts_path`; keyword search merges body/path rows, keeps the best rank per chunk id, sorts the merged rows, and only then applies the final limit.
- Why it matters / User impact: `openclaw memory search "2026-06-17-1649"` can find `memory/2026-06-17-1649.md` by filename/date token without prepending path text into the body index or weakening body BM25 relevance.
- Architecture / source-of-truth check: `memory_index_chunks` remains canonical; FTS tables are derived indexes; schema setup and full reindex publish own derived-index repair; search owns merging body/path keyword candidates before result limiting.
- Patch quality notes: the larger diff is from carrying the existing PR through the current canonical table migration, not from adding an unrelated feature. The repair marker is stored in `memory_index_meta` and keyed by the derived FTS table names, so legacy or unmarked indexes rebuild once while clean current indexes avoid repeated correlated scans over `UNINDEXED` FTS metadata columns.
- Related open PR scan: this updates my existing open PR #94114 for linked issue #94102. Sibling PR #94135 remains a maintainer selection signal; this PR now uses the current canonical `memory_index_*` schema rather than the stale pre-rebase table shape.

## Linked context

Closes #94102

Related #94114

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: memory search should find indexed memory files by filename/date tokens through the memory index/search path while preserving body BM25 ranking and avoiding repeated clean-index FTS virtual-table drift scans during schema setup.
- Real environment tested: isolated local OpenClaw CLI memory indexing/search on current head `2653f72688fe`, using a temporary OpenClaw home/workspace and `memorySearch.provider: "none"`. This update did not exercise an external OAuth/provider-backed live account because the changed code path is local memory indexing/search/schema repair, not provider auth or network I/O.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:

  ```bash
  node ./openclaw.mjs memory index --force
  node ./openclaw.mjs memory search "2026-06-17-1649" --json --max-results 3
  pnpm exec vitest run extensions/memory-core/src/memory/manager-db.test.ts extensions/memory-core/src/memory/manager-search.test.ts extensions/memory-core/src/memory/index.test.ts packages/memory-host-sdk/src/host/memory-schema.test.ts
  pnpm lint
  pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 packages/memory-host-sdk/src/host/memory-schema.ts packages/memory-host-sdk/src/host/memory-schema.test.ts
  git diff --check
  ```

- Evidence after fix:

  ```text
  HEAD=2653f72688fe
  $ node ./openclaw.mjs memory index --force
  Memory index updated (main).
  $ node ./openclaw.mjs memory search "2026-06-17-1649" --json --max-results 3
  {
    "results": [
      {
        "id": "2fc0e5052cd30a063fe293b5c42bfbf822471f493651c3926068b82dad822d8c",
        "path": "memory/2026-06-17-1649.md",
        "startLine": 1,
        "endLine": 2,
        "score": 0.9000039999839999,
        "textScore": 0.000003999984000064,
        "snippet": "OAuth token notes and Google testing mode details.\n",
        "source": "memory"
      }
    ]
  }

  Test Files  4 passed (4)
  Tests  98 passed (98)
  Duration  44.74s

  [oxlint:core] finished
  [oxlint:extensions] finished
  [oxlint:scripts] finished
  ```

- Observed result after fix: the current head returns `memory/2026-06-17-1649.md` for the CLI filename/date-token query and passes the canonical memory search/schema regressions, including date-token filename lookup, merge-before-limit behavior, unmarked FTS repair from `memory_index_chunks`, clean-index scan skipping after the repair marker is current, legacy source migration, and shadow path FTS publication.
- What was not tested: no external OAuth/provider-backed account, gateway session, provider auth flow, or network behavior was exercised for this update.

## Tests and validation

Which commands did you run?

```bash
node ./openclaw.mjs memory index --force
node ./openclaw.mjs memory search "2026-06-17-1649" --json --max-results 3
pnpm exec vitest run extensions/memory-core/src/memory/manager-db.test.ts extensions/memory-core/src/memory/manager-search.test.ts extensions/memory-core/src/memory/index.test.ts packages/memory-host-sdk/src/host/memory-schema.test.ts
pnpm lint
pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 packages/memory-host-sdk/src/host/memory-schema.ts packages/memory-host-sdk/src/host/memory-schema.test.ts
git diff --check
```

Target test files:

- `extensions/memory-core/src/memory/manager-db.test.ts`
- `extensions/memory-core/src/memory/manager-search.test.ts`
- `extensions/memory-core/src/memory/index.test.ts`
- `packages/memory-host-sdk/src/host/memory-schema.test.ts`

What regression coverage was added or updated?

- Body/path FTS candidates are merged before the requested result limit is applied, so a filename hit is not dropped when body matches fill the limit.
- `memory_index_chunks_fts` / `memory_index_chunks_fts_path` row-count mismatch or missing repair marker rebuilds derived rows from canonical `memory_index_chunks`.
- Clean FTS schema setup skips repeated `WHERE NOT EXISTS` virtual-table drift scans after the repair marker is current.
- Full shadow memory reindex publication copies `memory_index_chunks_fts_path`, so filename/date-token rows survive in-place reindex publish.
- Legacy memory stores without `source` columns migrate into canonical rows with the default `memory` source before rebuilding derived FTS rows.
- Merged keyword rows use `toSorted`, avoiding in-place ordering mutation while preserving rank order.

- Focused regression command after this patch: the four memory manager/schema test files above were run together against the committed head and passed 98 tests.
- Scenario locked in: canonical chunk rows remain the source of truth while body and path FTS tables are repaired/published separately, marked after repair, and merged before result limiting.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: these tests exercise the changed search merge/rank behavior, the schema-owner repair path, the repair-marker skip path, the legacy migration path, and the full reindex publish path directly without requiring provider embeddings or gateway-only startup proof.

## CI attribution

- Current proof is bound to PR head `2653f72688fe`.
- This update addresses both the contributor-side proof gap and the ClawSweeper reliability finding about repeated FTS virtual-table drift scans.
- Fresh remote checks should be evaluated against the current PR head.

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? `Yes`

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? `Yes`

Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? `No`

What is the highest-risk area?

Existing local memory SQLite stores may have derived FTS tables that are missing rows, use older FTS layouts, or were built before `source` existed on legacy memory tables.

How is that risk mitigated?

The repair treats `memory_index_chunks` as source of truth and rebuilds derived FTS rows when row counts differ or the repair marker is missing. After the marker is current, clean schema setup avoids repeated correlated scans over FTS virtual tables. It does not rewrite memory files, vectors, auth/config, provider state, or canonical chunk rows. Body-text FTS remains separate from path FTS, so filename lookup no longer changes body BM25 document-length normalization.

## Current review state

Which bot or reviewer comments were addressed?

- RF-001 / CLI terminal output: addressed with current-head CLI output showing `memory index --force` followed by JSON search returning `memory/2026-06-17-1649.md`.
- RF-002 / real behavior proof: addressed with the current-head CLI proof and targeted memory regression run in the Real behavior proof section above.
- RF-003 / proof freshness after rebase: addressed by binding the proof to `2653f72688fe`, the current PR head.
- RF-004 / persistent path FTS table and repair shape: intentionally disclosed as a maintainer design/upgrade-risk decision; `memory_index_chunks` remains canonical and both FTS tables remain derived.
- RF-005 / sibling PR #94135: disclosed as a maintainer selection signal; this PR presents the separate derived path FTS strategy on the canonical `memory_index_*` schema.
- RF-006 / remaining maintainer decision: the contributor-side current-head proof gap is addressed; the remaining choice is maintainer acceptance of the index strategy and upgrade-risk boundary.
- ClawSweeper commit review finding / repeated FTS drift scans: addressed by replacing per-initialization correlated virtual-table drift scans with a repair marker in `memory_index_meta`; unmarked legacy indexes rebuild derived FTS rows once, and current clean indexes skip the expensive scan path. ClawSweeper commit review was re-run after this change and is ready.

What is the next action?

Maintainers can compare this canonical-table approach with sibling PR #94135 and evaluate the normal remote checks on the current PR head.
target.author: zhangguiping-xydt

commands.reason: PR is not opted into ClawSweeper autofix or automerge
